coredata.dc:title: Biodistribution of carbon quantum dots derived from Caccinia macranthera extract via 99mTechnetium radiolabelling in rats
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei, Marzieh
Marouzi, Somayeh
Sadri, Kayvan
Sammak, Shirin
Darroudi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Even nowadays, 99mTechnetium (99mTc) is a radionuclide choice for diagnostics in nuclear medicine due to its unique properties and availability in many centers. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared using Caccinia macranthera (CM) plant extract via a hydrothermal method and we examined their cytotoxicity and biodistribution in rats. The CM-derived CQD was confirmed by XRD, FT-IR, DLS/Zeta potential, TEM, and UV–Vis analyses. The obtained results showed an average size of 5.6 nm, spherical morphology, optimal fluorescence, and quantum yield (QY) of 14.6 %, along with the presence of many hydrophilic functional groups (COOH, OH, and NH2) on the surface of CQDs. The toxicity of CQDs was assessed using MTT assay and ROS production tests in the CT26 cell line. Results showed that CQDs had an IC50 of ∼1000 μg/ml and exerted antioxidant effects in the 700 μg/ml concentration range. Also, in vivo studies in rats showed the bioaccumulation of 43.4 ± 5.38 in ID/g% for 99mTc-CQDs in the kidney tissue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Caccinia macranthera
Technetium
Reactive oxygen species

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Caccinia macranthera flower and sepal.
The preparation schematic of CQDs solution by powder of dried sepals of the CM plant.
FTIR spectrum of CQDs synthesized from CM plant.
Crystallographic structure of synthesized CQDs by X-ray diffraction.
The FESEM image (a & b) and EDX spectrum (c) of as-synthesized CQDs.
(a) TEM image, and (b) particle size histogram of the synthesized CQDs.
Zeta potential obtained from synthesized CQDs.
(a) Uv–Visible spectroscopy, and (b) bandgap energy of CQDs.
PL spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Cytotoxicity effect of CQDs on CT26 cancer cell line via the MTT assay, (b) and the ability of ROS neutralization by CQDs on CT26 cells.
The diagram illustrates the attachment of 99mTc-CQDs to the CT26 cells.
Scintigraphic images (anterior and posterior) taken after the intravenous injection of 99mTc- CQDs into a rat at 0 and 8 h.
Evaluation of the stability efficiency of 99mTc-CQDs at 0, 1, 4, and 24-hour intervals.
Investigating 99mTc-CQDs bioaccumulation in different organs of rats.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: %ID/g: Injected dose (%) per gram of organ ± SD (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Biodistribution of carbon quantum dots derived from Caccinia macranthera extract via 99mTechnetium radiolabelling in rats

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors are equally the first authors of this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Even nowadays, 99mTechnetium (99mTc) is a radionuclide choice for diagnostics in nuclear medicine due to its unique properties and availability in many centers. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully prepared using Caccinia macranthera (CM) plant extract via a hydrothermal method and we examined their cytotoxicity and biodistribution in rats. The CM-derived CQD was confirmed by XRD, FT-IR, DLS/Zeta potential, TEM, and UV–Vis analyses. The obtained results showed an average size of 5.6 nm, spherical morphology, optimal fluorescence, and quantum yield (QY) of 14.6 %, along with the presence of many hydrophilic functional groups (COOH, OH, and NH2) on the surface of CQDs. The toxicity of CQDs was assessed using MTT assay and ROS production tests in the CT26 cell line. Results showed that CQDs had an IC50 of ∼1000 μg/ml and exerted antioxidant effects in the 700 μg/ml concentration range. Also, in vivo studies in rats showed the bioaccumulation of 43.4 ± 5.38 in ID/g% for 99mTc-CQDs in the kidney tissue.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Throughout the last decade, the interest of scientist has been captivated by the small fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in the scale of <10 nm since their small size offers certain features in the quantum range, such as excellent biocompatibility and luminescence behavior, non-reactivity, and low toxicity [1]. These outstanding qualities were numerously reported to benefit a wide range of biological applications (in the field of bio-imaging [2,3], chemical sensor [4,5], disease diagnosis [6], biodistribution [7], photocatalysis [8], and fluorescence labeling [9]). The nanoparticle form of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared from natural carbon sources (due to their low cost, environmental friendliness, and availability), which has found a crucial stance in research communities [10]. The emergence of CQDs began with the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004. Compared to other fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, they contain remarkable fluorescent properties, the ability to tune broad excitation wavelengths, and excellent optical stability [11]. Therefore, this product can be used in biomedicine, sensors, and catalytic applications [12]. In this regard, due to its high surface-to-volume ratio and the presence of surface functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, ketone, etc), the functionalization of CQDs (biological molecules, organic substances, and polymers) can result in its applicability in the fields of treatment and diagnosis [13]. The synthesis of CQDs through “ green chemistry ” methods falls into the two general categories of “top-down and bottom-up” that implicate hydrothermal routes[14], microwave, ultrasonic processes, laser ablation of graphite, strong acid, and electrochemical oxidation [15], thermal decomposition of glycerol [16], exfoliation of graphite in organic solvent [17], and thermal carbonization of molecules [18]. In addition to selecting the appropriate synthesizing procedure for producing economical nanoparticles with low side effects, it is also necessary to choose the right natural carbon sources to promote the obtained biocompatibility. There are several reports on the synthesis of CQDs from natural and biodegradable sources [19] (orange [20], glucose [21], willow bark [22], coffee [23], sweet pepper [24], bell pepper [25]), and synthetic polymer [26]. Cancer has been recognized as a widespread and challenging disease in the field of medical research in recent years.

The Caccinia macranthera (CM) are listed as synonyms of CM in the Missouri catalog and Kew Botanical Gardens. This plant is native to Iran, Afghanistan, Pakistan, and India. The CM plant has a shrub form and the flowering season is in May and June. The uses of those fresh aerial parts, including flowers, are heart, lung, and liver tonic and expectorant [27]. In addition, it is not only an alternative tonic drug for syphilis, leprosy, and rheumatism in India, but it is also considered for its diuretic and sedative properties. The plant compounds isolated from flowers include pyrrolizidine alkaloid, a diester of retrorsine and benzoic acid [28]. According to the mentioned therapeutic properties of the CM plant and the lack of necessary scientific research in various therapeutic and diagnostic fields, especially on the nano-scale, this study evaluated bioaccumulation in rats. The extract obtained from the CM plant shown in Fig. 1 has been used under hydrothermal synthesis to prepare a CQD colloidal solution with bioimaging capabilities.

The investigations that were conducted achieved early detection techniques and effective and timely treatments by employing nanotechnology and imaging methods to some extent. The treatment of cancer patients implicates specific procedures, such as surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy [29]. Also, cases of advanced cancer are prescribed radiotherapy to reduce their rate of cell growth and destroy their cancerous cells, which is based on determining the necessary dosage to inhibit the progress of cancer. Methods based on combining nanomaterials with radioactive materials as smart medicinal systems, such as radionuclides, antibodies, polypeptides, liposomes, nanoparticles, microspheres, or injectable hydrogels, considered as an important principle in the therapeutic and diagnostic. Several studies reported the observance of a reduction in the side effects and toxicity of radioisotopes and nanoparticles through the design of targeted drug systems. Despite the advances in modern medicine, insensitivity to radioisotopes and other drugs is another challenge in the treatment of pancreatic carcinoma and Spongioblastoma cancers [30]. Therefore, conducting in vivo studies based on the bioaccumulation of medicinal substances is necessary to create efficient and effective platforms. Technetium radioisotope is one of the most widely used radiopharmaceuticals in nuclear medicine [31]. This ideal radiopharmaceutical product captivated the interest of many among the other diagnostic nuclear drugs due to its γ-radiation with a half-life of 6 h, low cost, and easy access. Various assessments reported labeling nanoparticles with a 99mTc radioisotope as a tracer in biodistribution research. At the same time, several other studies exhibited the labeling of nanoparticles with a 99mTc radioisotope as a tracer in biodistribution research [32]. The current research presents a facile and environmentally friendly method to prepare CQDs from CM plant extract. Subsequently, after gathering the necessary physicochemical data, the investigated CQDs applications in the efficient degradation of organic dyes, their cytotoxicity and antioxidant effects, and the biodistribution of 99mTc-CQDs in rats.

The fresh CM plant was collected from different regions of North Khorasan province in Iran, washed several times, and dried in a dark room. The required materials for investigating the cytotoxicity and type of reactive oxygen in synthesized CQDs, including 10 % FBS, Pen-strep (streptomycin and penicillin), and prepared RPMA culture medium (Merck, Germany), as well as 98 % (5،4-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-5،2-diphenyltetrazolium powder and DCFDA, were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. We also exerted the CT26 cell line (mouse colorectal cancer). Labeling CQDs for performing in vivo studies required the usage of hydrochloric acid, tin chloride, reducing agent (acetone), ketamine and xylazine 2 %, and silica gel paper, which were acquired from Merck, Germany. In addition, Na99mTcO4 was prepared using a molybdenum/technetium generator (Kimiya Broadcasting Co.).

The CM plant powder (1.0 g) was placed in an Erlenmeyer flask containing 100 mL of heated water (60 °C) to be extracted for a given period (2 h). According to the hydrothermal method, a Teflon-lined steel autoclave was used to place the suspension at a set temperature (180 °C) for 4 h. After that, the autoclave was gradually cooled to room temperature before centrifugation (10,000 rpm) and, therefore, its contents were filtered (0.22 μm pore size) to homogenize the nanoparticles and purify the synthesized CQDs colloidal solution. Finally, the synthesized CQDs colloidal solution was stored at 4 °C for use in the subsequent sections. Fig. 2 shows the schematic of the CQD colloidal solution synthesized by CM plant.

Quantum yield (QY) determines the ability of a photocatalyst to absorb photons and indicates how efficiently the absorbed photon is utilized for photocatalytic degradation. The fluorescence QY of the prepared CQDs is evaluated based on the slope method and calculated according to Eq. (1) [33].
(1)
Φ R
=
Φ S
Grad
R
Grad
S
η
R
2
η
S
2
Here, the subscripts S and R refer to quinine sulfate as the standard and CQDs as a sample.
Φ S
is for the QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (
Φ S
= 54 %
). Grad represents the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance, η is the refractive index of applied solvents, i.e., 0.1 M H2SO4 for quinine sulfate and water for CQDs [34].

To calculate the QY, six solutions were prepared of CQDs and quinine sulfate with different concentrations, and the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of each solution were measured (at
λ
ex
= 440 nm). The plot of fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance was drawn for quinine sulfate and CQDs, and the outcomes of
η
R
2
η
S
2
,
Grad
R
Grad
S
, and the QY of CQDs were observed at 0.995, 20.47, and 11 %, respectively.

The functional groups of prepared CQDs were determined by FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet, AVATAR 370). The crystallographic analysis relies on the wavelength properties of the X-ray apparatus used XRD, X'PertPro Cu Kα; λ = 0.154 nm (Panalytical Co, Holland). The nuclei of the atoms are situated within a crystalline lattice, positioned just a few angstroms apart. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (CE9500) was used to record the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, and the PL spectrum was determined through the utilization of a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL4600). Moreover, we distinguished CQDs' morphology and average size by applying field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images on a JCM microscope and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images on an AB LEO912 microscope. Next, to acquire the related Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum with a voltage acceleration of 20 kV, a zeta compact analyzer was used to perform the dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential measurements.

We assessed the viability of the CT26 cancer cell line in the vicinity of synthesized CQDs through the data of the MTT test, which was performed after culturing the cells in DMEM medium with FBS 10 % (CO2 atmosphere and 95 % humidified air at a temperature of 37 °C). The treatment of the CT26 cell line, in the count of 2 × 104 at each well of a 96 plate, implicated incubation in the CQDs concentration range of 16–1000 μg/ml for 48 h. Then, the MTT solution (with a concentration of 5 μg/ml in 20 μl) was added to the wells to be incubated for 4 h. Once the culture medium of each well was removed, DMSO (100 μl) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals and evaluate the cell viability rate. Cell viability was measured by comparing the absorbance ratio of cells in the treatment group to the control group by reading the absorbance of each well through an ELISA device (Biotek Instruments, USA). The treated cells with CQDs were incubated for 4 h after adding 10 mM of DCFDA to investigate the reactive oxygen species. The emission spectrum (fluorescence) was observed at 530 nm with the excitation wavelength of 480 nm.

99mMo/99mTc generator was exerted to provide the required technetium as a bioaccumulation tracer agent of CQDs, synthesized by hydrothermal method, for the labeling study. For this purpose, 2.0 ml of SnCl2 solution (2.0 mg) was prepared by using the reducing agent of 0.1 M hydrochloride. Then, the stock solution (100 μl of SnCl2 solution, 500 μl of 99mTc (5 mCi), and 1000 μl of synthesized CQDs were incubated at 1400 rpm/20 min to create a favorable CQDs labeling reaction, which was followed by evaluating the stability of labeling (99mTc-CQDs) by the performance of instant thin layer chromatography (ITLC) method.

The labeling quality of CQDs was evaluated through the results of instant thin-layer chromatography using silica gel sheets (15 × 5 cm) as the stationary phase and solvent reservoir, such as acetone, as the mobile phase. Colloidal stability (99mTc-CQDs) was evaluated under two different environmental conditions (human serum and PBS) at the time interval (0–24 h). Each prepared silica gel sheet was stained, placed in the solvent tank, and divided into three parts, respectively. Then, the sections were put in special RIA tubes to evaluate the radio-activate level in a γ-ray counter (Well Gamma Delsid, DL 100) [35].

The cell binding study of 99mTc-CQDs at two concentrations of 30 and 90 μg/ml was conducted with the application of a 6-well plate, while each well contained 1 × 106 mouse colorectal cancer cell lines for 72 h. The cell part and supernatant were washed by PBS to be separately transferred into the standard tubes of the gamma counter. The necessary calculations were done using the obtained data to determine the binding percentage of 99mTc-CQDs to the target cells [35].

We began the in vivo study by selecting the male Wistar rats (200–250 g) and dividing them into three groups to obtain the desired results and reduce the incidence of errors. 200 μl of 99mTc-CQD was administered through tail vein injection and of injection to sacrifice the desired rats after 1, 4, and 24 h. After transferring different organs into standard RIA tubes for weighing, the percentage of injected dose per gram of tissue ((%) ID/g) was measured by determining the amount of radioactivity through a gamma ray counter [35].

According to the rules of the ethics committee, the imaging of the selected rat was done under anesthesia (100 % ketamine and 2 % xylazine) to investigate the biodistribution of 99mTc-CQD by using a Siemens Double model of Gamma camera, Germany, as the posterior and anterior were captured at the time points of 0 and 8 h. Also, the static images were prepared with a magnification factor of 2 throughout the appointed period (10 min) [35].

The synthesized CQDs from the CM plant extract were produced using the hydrothermal method, followed by biophysical analysis to check the physicochemical properties and the animal study phase. In this regard, the synthesized CQDs were investigated via functional groups on the surface and how absorption wavelength was made through the FTIR technique [36]. Comparison of the FTIR absorption spectrum of CM plant extract and the absorption spectrum obtained from synthesized CQDs showed an increase in the number of functional groups and surface elements due to the reduction of particle size. The absorption spectrum obtained in the absorption range of 3200–3500 cm−1 showed the stretching vibration caused by OH, NH bonds as a broad peak at 3369 cm−1, which showed the movement to lower wavelengths compared to the template peak at 3399 cm−1 [37]. Recording the absorption peaks of 2929 and 2872 cm−1 for the synthesized CQDs can confirm the presence of methyl and methylene groups (symmetric and asymmetric CH stretches) on the surface [38]. In addition, the peaks at 1774 and 1711 cm−1, which indicated the CO band with stretching vibrations, compared to the 1730 cm−1 peak of the FTIR spectrum of CM plant extract, showed the changes caused by the carbonization process [39]. Furthermore, the observation of peaks at 1670 and 1600 cm−1 confirmed the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of COO, while the peaks at 1033 and 1074 cm−1 confirmed the stretching vibrations of the CO band of the alkoxy group [40]. Due to the presence of CH3 and OH functional groups and the uniform and long-term dispersion of CQDs based on the zeta potential results, it is possible to benefit from the prepared CQDs in the research fields of drug delivery and biosensors [41]. The FTIR analysis as shown in Fig. 3 , documented the aqueous solubility of CQDs due to the presence of COOH, OH, and NH2 (hydrophilic functional groups) [42].

The observed diffractions in XRD result with an FWHM of 20° in the graphite crystal plane 100 and 002 in Fig. 4 show the amorphous and semi-crystalline structures of CQDs [33] and carbonation of CM extract for the purity of CQDs [43].

The FESEM results obtained for CQDs morphology and size in Fig. 5 show a quasi-spherical structure on a very small scale with aggregation tendencies due to the high surface energy of CQDs. In addition to the calculated average particle size of 20.46 nm by the histogram curve, the EDX study conducted to evaluate the elemental and chemical composition of the prepared CQDs has confirmed the presence of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements in the weight ratios of 56.04, 35.76, and 8.2, respectively.

One of the valid analyses for the synthesized CQDs is measuring the morphology and size of the prepared particles using the TEM microscope analysis. The TEM microscope approach is performed by examining the metal core dimensions in the viewing plane using electrons based on single particles [44]. Therefore, the obtained quantitative results are shown as 2D images in Fig. 6 . These results indicated a semi-spherical morphology and an average size based on the histogram of 5.6 nm, which is consistent with other analytical methods obtained [45].

Analysis of a dispersed system to show the electrostatic degree of charged particles adjacent to each other (zeta potential method) is necessary for the colloidal evaluation of synthesized CQDs [35]. Therefore, the results obtained from the zeta potential of CQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method have confirmed the repulsive forces between particles with a value of −4.0 mV [45]. The magnitude of the negative charge of zeta potential is directly related to the amount of electrostatic repulsion between molecules. Therefore, increasing the dispersion of nanoparticles increases the system stabilized and zeta potential. In addition, the presence of electrostatic repulsion forces between water molecules and CQDs was observed through changes in the zeta potential distribution profile depending on the dispersion concentration of CQDs shown in Fig. 7
b. For example, at the stock concentration or dilution of 1.2 mg/ml of synthesized CQDs solution, minimum stability has observed. Also, the tendency to cluster in CQDs due to high energy content has caused dipole interaction and reduced conductivity among CQDs [46].

The optical properties of the synthesized CQDs were determined using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The maximum absorption wavelength (caused by induction of electron transfer between bonding orbitals (π → π*)) was observed at 283 nm, which can be attributed to electron transfer in CC bonds (aromatic sp2 hybridization) [40]. According to Fig. 8 , UV irradiation to the synthesized CQDs solution (wavelength 365 nm) was accompanied by green fluorescence emission. On the other hand, the band gap energy of this product is about 4.0 eV using Eq. (2) (Tauc equation) [47]. Therefore, the UV–Vis spectrophotometer results were consistent with the XRD pattern, and FTIR spectroscopy results emphasize the successful creation of CQDs in small sizes through the applied synthesis process.
(2)
α h ν =
A
h ν -
E g
n

Fluorescence emission is an interaction behavior towards environmental effects (pH, Temperature, etc.), and ligand interaction with vital macromolecules such as proteins is essential [48]. Also, synthesized nanomaterials with luminescent properties (fluorescence emission) are of interest to scientists as biosensors and luminesce probes [49]. Controlling and reducing the size on the nm scale can create a new feature in the synthesized nanoparticles famous for the quantum limit effect. Therefore, quantum limit effects are provided as a function of particle size, which is evident in CQDs. According to the synthesis method, the size of the synthesized particles, and the variety of carbon sources for the synthesis of CQDs, it is possible to observe the emission of fluorescence with various colors such as bright green, yellow, or blue through ultraviolet light radiation [50]. Consequently, the characteristics of fluorescence emission in CQDs can be controlled by monitoring factors such as size, shape, nature of edges, and band gaps. According to the dependence of the emission spectrum on the excitation wavelength, the solution of CQDs synthesized from CM plant extract (by hydrothermal method), with a concentration of 1000 μg/ml, was prepared by ultrasonic bath. According to Fig. 9 , the presented PL spectrum was analyzed in 360–470 nm. The maximum emission wavelength is 516 nm, which was emitted under the effect of the excitation wavelength of 430 nm. The excitation wavelengths applied at a distance of 10 nm revealed the shift of the fluorescence emission towards longer wavelengths, which was caused by the dependence of the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs on the excitation wavelength. The difference in the emission behavior of CQDs can confirm the distribution of functional groups on the surface and their small size consistent with the results of XRD, FTIR, and TEM images. Subsequently, the energy content of CQDs has increased due to their small size, which can reduce the fluorescence intensity and cause fluorescence quenching. In this regard, the reduction of electron layers and a red shift have been observed. Factors of size, stacking, sp3 sites, and conjugated ring structure have been engaged in changes in fluorescence emission [51]. The dependence of emission spectra on excitation wavelengths (luminescence phenomenon) is an ambiguous issue for scientists. Also, the phenomenon of luminescence is known for therapeutic and diagnostic applications. The QY compared to the standard reference such as quinine sulfate, 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4, as the emission efficiency of the optical spectrum of CQDs is known [52]. Therefore, the experimental fluorescence data of the synthesized CQD showed a calculated QY of 14.6 %. The findings suggest that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) produced from a natural carbon source (CM) through doping with elements like phosphorus, titanium, nitrogen, and others will be promising candidates for assessing biosensors, fluorescence probes, and photocatalysis [53–55].

The viability of the CT26 cell line after the treatment with synthesized CQDs by MTT test in the 16–1000 μg/ml concentration was investigated. Fig. 10
a displays the dependency of cell viability on the applied concentration and exhibits the obtained inhibitory concentration of cell growth (IC50) to be 1000 µg/ml. The cytotoxicity effects can be a function of certain factors, including synthesizing method, particle size, morphology, and the type of used cell line [56]. The CT26 cell line was subjected to the effects of synthesized CQDs to evaluate its level of ROS by using a microplate reader to obtain the DCF fluorescence emission (wavelength 530 nm) [57]. The antioxidant activity of the sample was evaluated due to the engagement radical inhibitory effect on the CT26 cell line at the concentration range of 700 μg/ml [58]. The groups under treatment, Fig. 10 b display the inducement of a decrease in fluorescence emission, indicating the effect of synthesized CQDs as an absorbent to reactive oxygen species from cell metabolism.

After receiving the necessary approvals from in vitro studies regarding the quality of synthesized products through a hydrothermal method, the CQDs were enhanced by technetium (99mTc) radiopharmaceutical to evaluate the obtained biodistribution in rats. For this purpose, the reducing agent (SnCl2, 100 μg/ml) was placed in the vicinity of 500 μg/ml of technetium pertechnetate 5 mCi, synthesized CQDs, and 1000 μg/ml to be incubated at room temperature for 20 min. The colloidal stability of labeled CQDs with 99mTc was determined through the outcomes of ITLC chromatography with stationary phase (silica gel strips) and mobile phase (acetone), implicating the stability of radio frequency in human serum and PBS environments throughout the time intervals of 0, 1, 4, and 24 h. The findings indicated that the radiofrequency stability of 99mTc-CQDs in human serum and PBS media after 24 h of labeling was 99 % and 47 %, respectively. The colloidal stability of CQDs-99mTc in human serum media was additionally validated to be strong. Moreover, the zeta potential assessment results at the value of −1.5 mV after labeling revealed changes in surface charge following the labeling of 99mTc-CQDs, which could confirm the effective binding of the 99mTc radiopharmaceutical to the surface of CQDs. Also, the particle size of CQDs-99mTc was found to be around 259 nm with a poly dispersion index (PDI) of 0.2 showed that labeled CQDs-99mTc has a good uniform distribution [59]. Table. 1 presents the findings from the study on the colloidal stability of 99mTc-CQDs in the mobile phase of acetone, as analyzed through ITLC chromatography.

We investigated the cell binding percentage of 99mTc-CQDs on the CT26 cell line at different concentrations (30 and 90 μg/ml) with three repetitions for each case and observed a more favorable state of cell attachment in low concentrations than the high volumes. After the 4 h of incubation and separation of supernatant from trypsinized cells, the amount of each cell attachment was subjected to radioactive ray reading by a gamma counter device [60]. Considering the impact of the small size and quasi-spherical morphology of CQD particles on increasing their accumulation tendency, the rate of cell attachment at the concentrations of 30 and 90 μg/ml was 70 % and 25 %, respectively. The process of evaluating the cell attachment of 99mTc-CQDs is outlined in a schematic illustration in Fig. 11 .

Determining the biological distribution of medicinal agents in treatment and diagnosis strategies is considered a key and necessary criterion. Therefore, nanoparticles with a concentrated purpose can be an engagement step in the treatment and early diagnosis of chronic and acute diseases and also reduce the side effects caused by chemical drugs with a wide range of effects. In this regard, the biodistribution assay of CQDs labeled with 99mTc was performed through intravenous (tail) injection of rats at time intervals (1, 4, and 24 h). The rats were killed at a definite time after the injection, and the rat organs were separately transferred to a standard tube (RIA) after being weighed. The basis of the biodistribution study was %ID/g (determining the percentage of injected dose per gram of tissue), and the results are shown in Table 2
. The highest bioaccumulation of 99mTc-CQD among other organs was in kidney tissue at the rate of 43.42 ± 5.380 after 4 h injection. Meanwhile, the bioaccumulation rate of 99mTc-CQD in two time periods of 1 and 24 h was still high in the kidneys. Despite the gradual reduction of bioaccumulation of 99mTc-CQDs in kidney tissue (34.52 ± 3.630) compared to other tissues, we observed a significant percentage of ID/g, which can be attributed to reabsorption (through the kidney system) due to spherical morphology and small size of particles. Therefore, the interaction of kidney tissue cells with 99mTc-CQDs had favorable colloidal stability and permeability [61].

In this section, the biodistribution of 99mTc-CQD was carried out with planar imaging capability of selected mice (randomly) by 99mTc-CQD injecting intravenously (tail). At first, using anesthetic drugs (ketamine and xylazine), the rat was anesthetized for a short time. A gamma camera was used to perform high-resolution imaging. The images obtained in two anterior and posterior modes showed the bioaccumulation of 99mTc-CQDs at 0 and 8 h [45]. The concentration or accumulation of 99mTc-CQDs concentrated in the kidneys is evident through scintigraphy images in Fig. 12
.

A review of numerous reports shows that nano-labels based on quantum dots are one of their most recent and prominent applications, especially in diagnostics and, more recently, in local radiotherapy when combined with radioisotopes. However, their biological behavior in different cellular or animal models and their functional activities in vivo have not yet been revealed. In 2019, Federico Duarte de Menezes and his team studied graphene-based quantum dots (GQDs) to assess their biological behavior using healthy mice and an endometriosis model simulating cancer-related tissue changes. The GQD nanoparticles, produced via an electrochemical method, showed safety, but some interactions with DNA were related to oxidative stress. Radiolabeling tests confirmed that stable radiolabeled GQDs could be efficiently produced (>90 %). In vivo studies found widespread distribution in healthy subjects, particularly in the liver (>26 %) and small intestine (>25 %), while in the endometriosis model, a notable uptake in the kidneys (>85 %) was observed [62]. Marouzi et al. 2021 studied CQDs synthesized from chia seeds to evaluate their biodistribution and cytotoxicity on the HEK293 cell line. They found an average particle size of 5.4 nm and a quantum yield of 17.8 %. Flow cytometry results showed a survival rate of 92.8 % for control cells and 91.7 % for cells treated with CQDs at 250 μg/mL, indicating a slight impact on cell survival. The accumulation rate of 99mTc-CQDs in kidney tissue was the highest at 31.82 ± 5.953 after 24 h [35]. Rahmani et al. (2019) presented a quick method for biosynthesizing fine spherical SPIONs via ultrasonic co-precipitation. The nanoparticles averaged 10 to 40 nm in size and exhibited superparamagnetic properties (Ms ∼ 52.5 emu/g). High zeta potential values were recorded for Cydonia oblonga/Fe3O4 particles, with 59.1 mV for SPIONS and 35.7 mV for 99mTc-SPIONS. Thin-layer chromatography tests showed a labeling yield of over 90 % and good long-term retention. Minimal cytotoxicity effects on A549 cell lines indicated potential for safe biological applications [63]. In the research, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from the extract of CM (a novel biodegradable and biocompatible source) have been validated as potential agents for therapy and diagnostics through physicochemical and in vivo techniques. Their excellent solubility in water due to their small size makes them ideal candidates for targeted drug delivery and doping studies in fluorescent tracers compared to other natural carbon sources.

Therefore, applying the hydrothermal synthesis method to produce biocompatible colloidal CQDs has been successful according to the results of different physicochemical techniques. Confirmation of size < 10 nm, spherical morphology through TEM, FESEM analysis methods, presence of amorphous structure in XRD evaluation, emission of green fluorescence (under ultraviolet light), and QY of 14.6 % as exceptional properties of synthesized colloidal CQDs have been. Moreover, the favorable medicinal potential of the synthesized CQDs for targeted and diagnostic therapies (∼700 μg/mL) is confirmed by their antioxidant effect and non-toxicity on CT26 cancer cells. The results of the in vivo study on kidney tissue showed the amount of 34.5 ± 3.63 during 24 h has the highest level of bioaccumulation among other organs consistent with the anterior and posterior images of mice. Therefore, these synthesized CQDs can be considered helpful candidates for treating and diagnosing kidney diseases.
This project was approved by the Ethics Committee of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (IR.MUMS.MEDICAL.REC.1399.663).

Marzieh Rezaei: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Somayeh Marouzi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kayvan Sadri: Supervision. Shirin Sammak: Formal analysis, Data curation. Majid Darroudi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: I am profoundly grateful to Azad University, the Research Sciences Unit, and Mashhad University of Medical Sciences for their technical support.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Rezaei, A. Hekmat, J. Chamani, K. Sadri, M. Darroudi, Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Trigonella foenum-graecum L seeds and their biodistribution in mice as an inorganic isotope label, Inorganic Chemistry Communications, 160 (2024) 111937.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots unraveling: Green synthesis, characterization, radiolabeling with 99mTc, in vivo behavior and mutagenicity
Mater. Sci. Eng. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.D. De Menezes, S.R.R. Dos Reis, S.R. Pinto, F.L. Portilho, F.d.V.C. e Mello, E. Helal-Neto, A.O.D.S. De Barros, L.M.R. Alencar, A.S. De Menezes, C.C. Dos Santos, Graphene quantum dots unraveling: Green synthesis, characterization, radiolabeling with 99mTc, in vivo behavior and mutagenicity, Materials Science and Engineering: C, 102 (2019) 405-414.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of 99mTc-labeled-Fe3O4 nanoparticles using Quince seeds extract and evaluation of their cytotoxicity and biodistribution in rats
J. Mol. Struct.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Rahmani, M. Gharanfoli, M. Gholamin, M. Darroudi, J. Chamani, K. Sadri, Green synthesis of 99mTc-labeled-Fe3O4 nanoparticles using Quince seeds extract and evaluation of their cytotoxicity and biodistribution in rats, Journal of Molecular Structure, 1196 (2019) 394-402.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Majid Darroudi is an associate professor of Nano-Sciences in the Department of Medical Biotechnology and Nanotechnology and head of the Nuclear Medicine Research Center (NMRC) at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (MUMS), Mashhad, Iran. His research interests are the synthesis of nanostructures and medical nanomaterials and using them in biomedical applications and wastewater treatment. He has published more than 300 peer-reviewed papers and +6 international book chapters in this field. He has been selected as among the top 1% of scientists in two different disciplines in 2020 by Web of Science™.
